claims.evidence_text: Air and soil growing degree days were calculated according to Method 1 of McMaster and Wilhelm (1997): ... and T base is equal to 0 °C (Baker et al., 1986; McMaster and Smika, 1988).
claims.claim_summary: Base temperature for wheat GDD calculation is 0 °C.

claims.evidence_text: wheat leaves should appear faster as soil temperature near the shoot apex increases, at least up to some optimum temperature (usually about 25–30 °C).
claims.claim_summary: Optimum temperature for wheat leaf appearance is about 25–30 °C.

claims.evidence_text: the pooled phyllochrons for both temperature treatments within a planting date ranged from 68 (PD1, 1998) to 77 (PD1, 1999) air GDD
claims.claim_summary: Spring wheat phyllochron (air-GDD leaf appearance interval) ranged 68–77 GDD across planting dates.

claims.evidence_text: A mean phyllochron of 74 air growing degree days (Table) would suggest about 2·7 (PD1), 2·3 (PD2) and 1·8 (PD3) more leaves appearing in the +3C treatments.
claims.claim_summary: Mean spring wheat phyllochron about 74 air GDD per leaf.

claims.evidence_text: Two centimetres of soil were placed on top of the seeds and thermocouples, resulting in a 2 cm seed depth.
claims.claim_summary: Spring wheat sown at 2 cm seed depth.

claims.evidence_text: Seed spacing was 2 cm, giving a density of 250 seeds m –2.
claims.claim_summary: Seeding density of 250 seeds per m2.

claims.evidence_text: fertilizer was broadcast at a rate of 39 kg N ha –1 and 22 kg P ha –1.
fertilizer was broadcast at a rate of 39 kg N ha –1 and 22 kg P ha –1.
Emergence was greater than 90 % for all treatments.
